evidence: API route handlers consistently use try-catch with console.error + NextResponse.json({ error }, { status }) — e.g., transfer/route.ts, dispatch/route.ts, route-handlers.js all follow the same shape

UI components in server-settings/ consistently use try-catch with setError(message) for user-facing feedback — overview-section.tsx, channels-section.tsx, danger-zone-section.tsx all follow this pattern
useDmList.refresh (use-dm-list.ts:43-72) silently drops non-ok HTTP responses — if res.ok is false, no error is set, no log emitted, caller cannot distinguish failure from empty result
DangerZoneSection.fetchMembers (danger-zone-section.tsx:45-47) has an empty catch block with a '// Silently fail' comment — members list fails without any user or developer feedback
ChannelsSection.fetchChannels (channels-section.tsx:44-51) silently drops non-ok responses — only catches thrown exceptions, ignoring HTTP-level errors

persistMessage and updateMessage in dispatch/route.ts (lines 312-338) log errors but never throw — callers proceed as if persistence succeeded, meaning a stream can report success while the message was never saved
socket.ts fetchToken (lines 11-20) returns null for both network errors and auth failures — useSocket callers cannot distinguish 'not authenticated' from 'network down' from 'server-side rendering'
ChannelsSection.handleReorder (channels-section.tsx:141-149) catches errors and silently refetches — no user feedback that reorder failed

strengths: API route handlers follow a uniform pattern: try-catch wrapping DB operations with console.error + structured JSON error response with appropriate HTTP status codes
UI components in server-settings/ consistently use setError() state to surface errors to users, with a shared pattern of err instanceof Error ? err.message : fallback
JSON body parsing across all API routes consistently catches parse failures and returns 400 with { error: 'Invalid JSON body' }
The dispatch route handler carefully distinguishes AbortError (504) from general errors (502) in webhook calls
error_consistency.issue_character: The codebase has two well-established error handling conventions (API routes and UI components), but client-side data fetching hooks and some UI fetch operations silently drop non-ok HTTP responses, creating a blind spot where failures produce no user feedback and no developer diagnostics.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The API layer and the settings UI components demonstrate consistent, predictable error handling — a developer working there would know what to expect. However, the client-side hooks (useDmList, useSocket) and several fetch callbacks in UI components silently swallow non-ok responses, creating a distinct subsystem where error behavior is unpredictable. This is not just a local issue — it affects the entire data-fetching layer of the frontend. The mix of 'sometimes errors surface, sometimes they vanish' pulls the score below 80 into the mid-70s, where a developer would encounter enough inconsistency that they cannot fully trust error handling conventions learned from one area.

issues.summary: useDmList.refresh silently drops non-ok HTTP responses without logging or surfacing error
evidence: Lines 43-72: when res.ok is false, execution falls through the if block with no else — no error state is set, no console.error logged, and isLoading transitions to false as if the fetch succeeded with zero results
issues.suggestion: Add an else branch after the res.ok check: log the status/error and either set an error state on the hook's return value or re-throw so the catch block handles it consistently with the thrown-exception path

issues.summary: DangerZoneSection.fetchMembers has an empty catch block that silently swallows all errors
evidence: Lines 45-47: catch block is empty with only a '// Silently fail' comment — a network error, JSON parse error, or server 500 all vanish without any user or developer feedback, while sibling functions in the same component (handleTransfer, handleDelete) properly surface errors via setError()
issues.suggestion: At minimum add console.error for developer diagnostics; ideally set error state so the user knows members could not be loaded (e.g., 'Could not load members for transfer')

issues.summary: ChannelsSection.fetchChannels silently ignores non-ok HTTP responses
evidence: Lines 44-51: only processes data when res.ok is true; when res.ok is false, no error message is set — loading spinner disappears and user sees empty channel list with no explanation
issues.suggestion: Add an else branch to set error state when res.ok is false, consistent with the component's other fetch handlers (handleCreate, handleDelete, handleSaveEdit) which all surface errors via setError()

issues.summary: persistMessage and updateMessage in dispatch route log errors but never propagate them to callers
evidence: Lines 301-338: both helper functions log errors via console.error but return void — the main handler at line 240 returns { ok: true } even if the message was never persisted, meaning a successful-looking webhook dispatch can silently lose messages
issues.suggestion: Have persistMessage and updateMessage throw on failure (or return a success boolean) so the main handler can reflect persistence failures in its response — at minimum for the initial persistMessage call which is critical to message durability

issues.summary: getSocket and fetchToken return null for three distinct failure modes with no way for callers to distinguish them
evidence: fetchToken (lines 11-20) returns null for network errors, non-ok responses, and missing token field; getSocket (lines 26-61) returns null for SSR context, auth failure, and network failure — useSocket (use-socket.ts) receives null and cannot provide meaningful diagnostics to the user
issues.suggestion: Return a discriminated result from getSocket indicating the failure reason (e.g., { socket: null, reason: 'auth_failed' | 'network_error' | 'ssr' }) so callers can provide appropriate feedback — or at minimum add console.warn with the failure context

issues.summary: ChannelsSection.handleReorder catches errors and silently refetches without notifying the user
evidence: Lines 141-149: on reorder failure, the catch block calls fetchChannels() to revert optimistic update but never calls setError() — user sees channels snap back to original order with no explanation, while sibling handlers (handleCreate, handleDelete, handleSaveEdit) all surface errors
issues.suggestion: Add setError('Failed to reorder channels') before the fetchChannels() call in the catch block, matching the error reporting pattern used by sibling handlers in the same component

root_causes: Client-side fetch patterns lack a shared convention for handling non-ok HTTP responses — each component/hook independently decides whether to check res.ok and what to do when it fails
No shared fetch wrapper or error-handling utility for frontend API calls, leading to inconsistent error surfacing across hooks and components
likely_symptoms: silent_non_ok_response_dm_list and silent_non_ok_channels_fetch are symptoms of the missing fetch error convention
silent_catch_fetch_members and reorder_silent_fallback are symptoms of inconsistent error surfacing within the same component family
